name: Glendive
short_description: BLM public land near Glendive offering accessible rockhounding for agate, petrified wood, and jasper. Gentle terrain and no permit required make this an ideal spot for beginners exploring Montana's...
description:
The Glendive area sits within Montana's famous Fort Union Formation, a geological zone rich with fossils and semi-precious stones. This BLM land offers excellent opportunities for collectors seeking agate nodules, colorful jasper, and petrified wood specimens weathering out of badland exposures. The landscape is characteristic of eastern Montana's rolling prairie and eroded badland country, where millions of years of erosion have exposed layers of ancient sedimentary rocks. Collectors will find the terrain relatively approachable with gentle slopes and well-defined washes where specimens naturally concentrate. The area attracts rockhounds year-round but is particularly rewarding in spring and fall when weather is mild and visibility is best.

The geology here reflects the Paleocene epoch, roughly 60 million years ago, when the Fort Union Formation was deposited in fluvial and lacustrine environments. These conditions produced silica-rich minerals that crystallized into agates and chalcedony as groundwater percolated through volcanic ash and sedimentary layers. Petrified wood is common because the region supported lush forests before becoming the semi-arid badlands visible today. The gentle slopes mean you can walk access areas without extreme exertion, and specimens are found both on surface weathered exposures and in eroded gully bottoms.

Expect to spend several hours exploring multiple small washes and hillside outcrops. The best material is typically found where badland formations have eroded, creating natural sorting that concentrates heavier stones. Bring plenty of water as shade is limited and the terrain offers minimal protection. The generously sized BLM holding here means you have room to spread out and explore without crowding.
history: The Fort Union Formation in this region was deposited during the Paleocene epoch as rivers and lakes filled the Williston Basin following the retreat of the Western Interior Seaway. The agate, jasper, and petrified wood found here crystallized from silica-rich fluids percolating through volcanic ash layers and organic-rich sediments, a process that began tens of millions of years ago and continues today as erosion exposes fresh material.
tips: Search the bottoms and banks of intermittent washes where water has naturally sorted and concentrated heavier agate and jasper specimens - look for colorful bands of chalcedony and translucent nodules. Use a hand lens to examine small pieces on exposed badland faces; petrified wood often shows grain structure and is lighter in color than surrounding rock. Bring a 5-gallon bucket for transport since the 25-pound daily limit can accumulate quickly with quality specimens, and wear sturdy boots as the badland terrain has sharp edges and loose footing.
terrain_notes: Elevation 2081 feet with gentle rolling slopes typical of Montana badlands. Terrain consists of eroded sedimentary exposures, sparse grass, and sagebrush with occasional small washes. Walking distances from parking are typically under 1 mile to productive areas. Footing is uneven with loose rock and badland scree common; terrain is accessible but requires attention to footing. No shade available.
best_season: April - June and September - October. Spring offers mild temperatures and occasional light rains that help expose fresh specimens; early summer mornings are productive before heat increases. Fall brings cooler temperatures and lower humidity ideal for extended fieldwork. Winter and midsummer are less comfortable due to cold/snow and extreme heat respectively.
written_directions: From Glendive, head south or east on BLM access roads in the Dawson County badlands area. Specific access points vary; consult BLM Glendive Field Office maps or local rockhounding resources for current road conditions and optimal collecting areas. Standard high-clearance vehicle recommended; some pull-outs and informal parking areas exist along working roads through badland terrain. GPS navigation is essential as the landscape offers limited landmarks.
road_conditions: Improved dirt roads with potential washboard surface; some rough washed-out sections after heavy rain. High-clearance vehicle recommended; passenger cars acceptable on main BLM access routes but may struggle with side roads.
parking_notes: Informal pull-outs and turnouts along BLM roads; capacity varies. No developed parking lot. Parking directly along road verges is typical; ensure full vehicle clearance from travel lanes.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Collect only loose material; do not break specimens from bedrock.
rules:

Collect loose material only
nearest_services: Glendive, Montana - approximately 5-10 miles north. Full services including gas, restaurants, groceries, and lodging available in town.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain accessible to most hikers with basic fitness. Uneven badland footing requires sturdy footwear but no technical climbing or scrambling. Loose rock and sharp badland scree common; no wheelchair accessibility. Short walking distances (typically under 1 mile) make this suitable for families and moderate mobility.

faq.question: What gems and minerals can you find rockhounding near Glendive, Montana?
faq.answer: The Glendive area is known for agate, jasper, and petrified wood found in badland exposures of the Fort Union Formation. Agate nodules display chalcedony banding, jasper comes in varied colors including red and brown, and petrified wood specimens retain visible grain structure. All three are accessible to collectors on BLM land without permits.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound on BLM land near Glendive?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on BLM land near Glendive. Personal use collecting is allowed under FLPMA regulations with a 25-pound daily limit. Commercial collection is prohibited. Simply contact the BLM Glendive Field Office if you have questions about specific collecting areas.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect agate and petrified wood near Glendive?
faq.answer: April through June and September through October are ideal. Spring offers mild temperatures and fresh exposures after winter weathering; fall provides cool conditions perfect for extended fieldwork. Midsummer heat and winter snow make those seasons less comfortable for collecting in the badlands.

faq.question: How hard is it to find quality agate specimens in the Glendive badlands?
faq.answer: Finding quality agate is moderate difficulty - they are fairly common in badland washes and outcrops, but locating gem-quality translucent pieces requires patience and a trained eye. Spending 2-3 hours exploring multiple small washes typically yields several keeper specimens along with smaller fragments. Beginner rockhounders will be successful here.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding at Glendive?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, a 5-gallon bucket or small backpack for the 25-pound daily collection limit, a hand lens for examining small specimens, and plenty of water - the badlands offer no shade. A GPS unit or smartphone with offline maps helps navigate the terrain. Gloves are optional but protect against sharp badland rock. A small cold chisel is handy for extracting specimens but breaking bedrock is prohibited.

faq.question: Is Glendive rockhounding suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes - Glendive is excellent for beginners and families. The terrain is gently sloped and easily walkable without technical skills. No permit is required, and agate and petrified wood are common enough that children ages 6 and up can find specimens within short distances from parking. The 25-pound daily limit ensures manageable hauls for young collectors.

meta_title: Glendive Rockhounding - Montana Agate & Petrified Wood
meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and petrified wood on BLM land near Glendive, Montana. No permit required. 25 lbs per day. Beginner-friendly badland terrain.
